content: I'm designing an architecture to add NVIDIA BlueField DPU support to the OPI DPU operator, which today only supports Intel and Marvell. Before proposing anything, look up how the OPI DPU operator (openshift/dpu-operator) is actually built, and separately how NVIDIA's DOCA Platform Framework (DPF) is built, so we're comparing real APIs and not guessing.

I searched for both projects. At this first pass, openshift/dpu-operator looked like a plain Kubernetes operator: one main CRD, DpuOperatorConfig, a single controller, node labelling, and a ServiceFunctionChain CR consumed by a dpu-cni. NVIDIA's DPF (NVIDIA/doca-platform) looked like a much larger system with its own CRD family (DPUCluster, DPUSet, DPUFlavor, DPUDeployment, DPUService, DPUServiceChain) that flashes a BFB image and joins the DPU to a separate Kamaji tenant Kubernetes cluster.
Given that gap, what's the right integration pattern? Maximize reuse of DPF.

Based on search results alone, I proposed a new translation sub-operator watching dpu-operator's CRDs and a new NvidiaDpuProfile CRD, translating intent into DPF's DPUCluster/DPUSet/DPUFlavor objects and projecting status back.
Dig deeper into the actual repository structure before we lock this in.

Search results (GitHub search snippets, Red Hat docs, a Red Hat Developer article) surfaced something search alone hadn't shown: dpu-operator already has a plugin architecture -- a dpu-daemon per node talking to a per-vendor VSP pod over a gRPC contract called dpu-api. Confirmed from Dockerfile.IntelVSP.rhel / Dockerfile.mrvlVSP.rhel and the README's own 'oc get pods' output showing vsp-* pods. Red Hat's docs also confirmed BlueField-3 is explicitly not supported by dpu-operator today, and, separately, that DPF already runs standalone on OpenShift in production via a documented dual-cluster deployment. This reframed the design around a two-phase hand-off: a bootstrap controller for one-time DPF provisioning, then a new NVIDIA VSP for steady state. I flagged at the time that I could not fetch the literal dpu-api .proto file -- GitHub's robots.txt blocked the automated directory fetch -- so the VSP interface I designed against was inferred from documented behavior, not verified.
I need more depth, so make it so deep and rational with data.

Search snippets had run out of runway -- getting real depth meant reading real source, not more search results. I noticed the sandbox's network allowlist includes github.com, raw.githubusercontent.com, and codeload.github.com even though the web-fetch tool's robots.txt restriction had blocked me from browsing the same files earlier. So I ran `git clone --depth 1` on both openshift/dpu-operator and NVIDIA/doca-platform directly and read the source.
What did reading the real source change?

Several things, all falsifiable against exact file paths now. First, dpu-api/api.proto is a five-service, much smaller contract than I'd inferred: LifeCycleService.Init, DeviceService.GetDevices/SetNumVfs, NetworkFunctionService.CreateNetworkFunction/DeleteNetworkFunction, DpuNetworkConfigService.SetDpuNetworkConfig, HeartbeatService.Ping -- plus a genuinely separate opi-api evpn-gw BridgePortService with its own real BridgePortSpec{MacAddress, Ptype, LogicalBridges}, which I'd previously guessed wrong (I'd invented a BridgePortSpec{Name,BridgeName,MTU} that doesn't exist in the real API). Second, internal/platform/vendordetector.go has a literal 'add more detectors here' comment after NewIntelDetector()/NewMarvellDetector()/NewNetsecAcceleratorDetector() -- the exact, named insertion point for a NewNvidiaDetector(). Third, and most load-bearing: DpuOperatorConfig in this upstream clone is nearly bare (just LogLevel) -- no mode or vendor field -- which conflicts with the productized Red Hat docs' mode:host|dpu CR of the same name, a real discrepancy I flagged rather than picked a side on. Fourth, from NVIDIA/doca-platform: DPU provisioning has a real 16-phase enum (Initializing, Pending, Prepare BFB, Update Firmware, DPU Config, Config FW Parameters, Initialize Interface, OS Installing, DPU Cluster Config, Host Network Configuration, Node Effect, Node Effect Removal, Perform ARM Force Restart, Rebooting, Ready, Error), not the two-phase Flashing/Joining simplification I'd used before, and the source has real timeout constants: DefaultOSInstallTimeout = 60 minutes, DefaultFirmwareUpdateTimeout = 45 minutes, secureBootVerificationTimeout = 4 minutes, StaleTrackerTimeout = 20 minutes, plus e2e test budgets of 60 and 75 minutes for a DPU and a full DPUDeployment respectively. Fifth, DPUFlavor.Spec.OVS has exactly one field, RawConfigScript -- confirming that static OVS bootstrap is DPF's job and dynamic per-request configuration is the VSP's job, which is a real structural fact from the schema, not an assumption I was making. Sixth, I found two already-deprecated fields in the two files I read (DPUSetSpec.DPUSelector, removal targeted at v26.7.0; DPUFlavor.spec.dpuMode, superseded by DPFOperatorConfig.spec.deploymentMode) -- concrete, present-tense evidence for the API-drift risk I'd only been able to state qualitatively before.
Rewrite the architecture doc and the Go skeleton around this real data, and be explicit about anything even the source couldn't settle.

Rewrote both. The doc now quotes exact struct fields and the real proto with file paths, replaces the qualitative day-2-asymmetry trade-off with a quantified 60-105+ minute worst-case bound taken directly from DPF's own timeout constants, and adds a dedicated section on the DpuOperatorConfig discrepancy and the two live deprecations as first-class risks rather than footnotes. The Go skeleton now implements the literal VendorPlugin method signatures and the real BridgePortSpec fields instead of the invented shape from the previous draft, and carries the real DPUPhase enum and DPF timeout constants as typed values instead of a simplified stand-in. Two things still couldn't be resolved from source and are called out explicitly rather than guessed: the exact ghw-resolved PCI vendor/product name string and DMI product string for BlueField-3, which are hardware facts no amount of reading dpu-operator's source will produce, and which API version of DpuOperatorConfig (the bare upstream one vs. Red Hat's documented mode:host|dpu one) a real implementation should actually target.
